sanskrit_devanagari:
त्यक्त्वा कर्मफलासङ्गं नित्यतृप्तो निराश्रयः ।
कर्मण्यभिप्रवृत्तोऽपि नैव किञ्चित्करोति सः ॥ २० ॥
sanskrit_iast:
tyaktvā karma-phalāsaṅgaṁ nitya-tṛpto nirāśrayaḥ |
karmaṇy abhipravṛtto 'pi naiva kiñcit karoti saḥ || 20 ||

anvaya.meaning: without dependence / without shelter (in any external)

translations.verbatim_quote: Abandoning all attachment to the results of his activities, ever satisfied and independent, he performs no fruitive action, although engaged in all kinds of undertakings.
Abandoning all attachment to the results of his activities, ever satisfied and independent, he performs no fruitive action, although engaged in all kinds of undertakings.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 874 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: This freedom from the bondage of actions is possible only in Kṛṣṇa consciousness, when one is doing everything for Kṛṣṇa. A Kṛṣṇa conscious person acts out of pure love for the Supreme Personality of Godhead, and therefore he has no attraction for the results of the action.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.20 With the help of the above-mentioned wisdom, tyaktva, having given up the idea of agentship; and phala-asangam, attachment to the results of action; he who is nitya-trptah, ever-trptah, ever-contented, i.e. has no hankering for objects; and nirasrayah, dependent on nothing-.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-20-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as the integrated portrait: the actor whose mind is released from fruit-attachment, whose contentment is intrinsic and constant (not contingent on outcomes), and who is dependent on no external support — such an actor's full engagement in action is, metaphysically, akarma. The verse is 4.18 made operational.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.20 Whoever performs actions, renouncing attachment to their fruits and is satisfied with the eternal, i.e., satisfied with his own self, and dependent on none, i.e., devoid of dependence on transient Prakrti (body and external nature) — such a perosn, even though fully engaged in actions, does not…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-20-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as the description of the karma-yogin established in the practice: the one who, having relinquished fruit-attachment, rests in the inner contentment of devotion to the Lord and does not seek refuge in any worldly support. Such a one's action, however fully performed, does not bind because it is offered, not appropriated.

positions.rendering: always engaged in work, [yet] he is doing nothing
his fully-engaged action is metaphysically akarma — non-binding because issuing from non-doership
his action does not bind because it is offered, not appropriated by an ego that claims authorship

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three preserve the paradox: full engagement + no doing. Shankara grounds the paradox in non-doership of the Self; Ramanuja grounds it in offered action without ego-appropriation. Both readings are coherent with 2.47 (no claim to authorship of fruits) and 4.18 (the seer's paradoxical relation to action).

literal_meaning: Having abandoned attachment to the fruits of action, ever content, dependent on nothing — though fully engaged in action, he does not act at all.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse is the operational portrait of 4.18's seer. Three qualities are named: (1) tyaktvā karma-phalāsaṅgam — having released attachment to fruits (echoing 2.47); (2) nitya-tṛpta — ever-content; (3) nirāśraya — taking shelter in nothing external. Such a one, though fully engaged in action (karmaṇy abhipravṛttaḥ), does not act at all in the binding-sense. The verse anchors the chapter's practice in coherence with the 2.47-2.48 doctrine and prepares 4.21-4.23.
